title: शान्ति पाठ
devanagariText:
॥ श्री शान्ति पाठः ॥

ॐ द्यौः शान्तिरन्तरिक्षँ शान्तिः
पृथिवी शान्तिरापः शान्तिरोषधयः शान्तिः।
वनस्पतयः शान्तिर्विश्वे देवाः शान्तिर्ब्रह्म शान्तिः
सर्वँ शान्तिः शान्तिरेव शान्तिः सा मा शान्तिरेधि॥१॥

ॐ शान्तिः शान्तिः शान्तिः॥

॥ अन्य शान्ति मन्त्राः ॥

ॐ सह नाववतु। सह नौ भुनक्तु।
सह वीर्यं करवावहै।
तेजस्वि नावधीतमस्तु मा विद्विषावहै॥
ॐ शान्तिः शान्तिः शान्तिः॥२॥

ॐ पूर्णमदः पूर्णमिदं पूर्णात्पूर्णमुदच्यते।
पूर्णस्य पूर्णमादाय पूर्णमेवावशिष्यते॥
ॐ शान्तिः शान्तिः शान्तिः॥३॥

ॐ असतो मा सद्गमय।
तमसो मा ज्योतिर्गमय।
मृत्योर्मा अमृतं गमय॥
ॐ शान्तिः शान्तिः शान्तिः॥४॥

ॐ सर्वे भवन्तु सुखिनः सर्वे सन्तु निरामयाः।
सर्वे भद्राणि पश्यन्तु मा कश्चिद्दुःखभाग्भवेत्॥
ॐ शान्तिः शान्तिः शान्तिः॥५॥

ॐ सर्वेषां स्वस्तिर्भवतु। सर्वेषां शान्तिर्भवतु।
सर्वेषां पूर्णं भवतु। सर्वेषां मंगलं भवतु॥
ॐ शान्तिः शान्तिः शान्तिः॥६॥

ॐ भद्रं कर्णेभिः शृणुयाम देवाः।
भद्रं पश्येमाक्षभिर्यजत्राः।
स्थिरैरंगैस्तुष्टुवाँसस्तनूभिः
व्यशेम देवहितं यदायुः॥
ॐ शान्तिः शान्तिः शान्तिः॥७॥

॥ इति शान्ति पाठः सम्पूर्णः ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Shanti Pathah ||

Om Dyauh Shantir-antarikshag Shantih
Prithivi Shantir-apah Shantir-oshadhayah Shantih.
Vanaspatayah Shantir-Vishve Devah Shantir-Brahma Shantih
Sarvag Shantih Shantir-eva Shantih Sa Ma Shantir-edhi.||1||

Om Shantih Shantih Shantih.

|| Anya Shanti Mantrah ||

Om Saha Navavatu. Saha Nau Bhunaktu.
Saha Viryam Karavavaahai.
Tejasvi Navadhitam-astu Ma Vidvishavahai.
Om Shantih Shantih Shantih.||2||

Om Purnamadah Purnamidam Purnat-purnam-udachyate.
Purnasya Purnam-adaya Purnam-evavashishyate.
Om Shantih Shantih Shantih.||3||

Om Asato Ma Sadgamaya.
Tamaso Ma Jyotirgamaya.
Mrityor-ma Amritam Gamaya.
Om Shantih Shantih Shantih.||4||

Om Sarve Bhavantu Sukhinah Sarve Santu Niramayah.
Sarve Bhadrani Pashyantu Ma Kashchid-duhkha-bhag-bhavet.
Om Shantih Shantih Shantih.||5||

Om Sarvesham Svastir-bhavatu. Sarvesham Shantir-bhavatu.
Sarvesham Purnam Bhavatu. Sarvesham Mangalam Bhavatu.
Om Shantih Shantih Shantih.||6||

Om Bhadram Karnebhih Shrunuyama Devah.
Bhadram Pashyema-akshabhir-yajatrah.
Sthirair-angais-tushtuvansas-tanubhih
Vyashema Devahitam Yad-ayuh.
Om Shantih Shantih Shantih.||7||

|| Iti Shanti Pathah Sampurnah ||
hindiMeaning:
आकाश में शान्ति हो, अन्तरिक्ष में शान्ति हो, पृथ्वी पर शान्ति हो, जल में शान्ति हो, औषधियों में शान्ति हो, वनस्पतियों में शान्ति हो, सभी देवों में शान्ति हो, ब्रह्म में शान्ति हो, सब में शान्ति हो, शान्ति ही शान्ति हो, वह शान्ति मुझे प्राप्त हो।

वह (परमात्मा) हम दोनों (गुरु-शिष्य) की एक साथ रक्षा करें। हम दोनों का एक साथ पोषण करें। हम दोनों एक साथ वीर्य (शक्ति) प्राप्त करें। हमारा अध्ययन तेजस्वी हो। हम परस्पर द्वेष न करें।

वह पूर्ण है, यह पूर्ण है। पूर्ण से पूर्ण उत्पन्न होता है। पूर्ण से पूर्ण निकालने पर भी पूर्ण ही शेष रहता है।

हमें असत्य से सत्य की ओर ले चलो। अन्धकार से प्रकाश की ओर ले चलो। मृत्यु से अमृतत्व की ओर ले चलो।

सभी सुखी हों, सभी निरोग हों, सभी का कल्याण हो, किसी को भी दुःख प्राप्त न हो।

हे देवगण! हम कानों से कल्याणकारी सुनें, नेत्रों से कल्याणकारी देखें। स्थिर अंगों और शरीर से स्तुति करते हुए जो देव-निर्धारित आयु है, उसे भोगें।
benefits: Traditionally invoked to establish universal peace and harmony in the environment and within oneself
Traditional Vedic prayer recited at the beginning and end of all spiritual practices
Traditionally recited for removal of the three types of suffering: adhyatmika, adhibhautika, and adhidaivika
Invokes physical health, mental peace, and spiritual tranquility
Invokes blessings for the welfare of all beings in the universe
Purifies the atmosphere and removes negativity from the surroundings
description: Among the most cherished prayers to Lord Vishnu, Shanti Path - Om Dyauh Shanti (शान्ति पाठ) stands as a sacred prayer. Rooted in Yajurveda (36.17) and Upanishads, this composition carries deep spiritual significance. Chanting this stotra with devotion is said to grant physical health, mental peace, and spiritual tranquility as well as purify the atmosphere and remove negativity from the surroundings. Additionally, it is known to create universal peace and harmony in the environment and within oneself. At 7 verses long, the recitation takes roughly 5 minutes, making it accessible for regular devotional use. On this page, you can read the complete Shanti Path - Om Dyauh Shanti in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Shanti Path (Om Dyauh Shanti) in Sanskrit with Hindi meaning and transliteration. Vedic peace prayers for universal harmony and well-being.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Shanti Path?
seoFaqs.answer: The Shanti Path (शान्ति पाठ — 'Peace Recitation') is the principal collection of Vedic peace mantras (shanti mantras) recited at the beginning and end of every traditional Hindu spiritual practice — puja, havan, Vedanta study session, kirtan, satsang, marriage, housewarming, mundane samskaras, and at the close of every Upanishad. It opens with the supreme universal-peace mantra of the Yajurveda 36.17 — Om Dyauh Shantir-antarikshag Shantih, prithivi shantir-apah shantir-oshadhayah shantih — invoking peace in the heavens, in the atmosphere, on earth, in the waters, in the herbs, in the trees, in all the devas, in Brahman and in everything. The Shanti Path then collects six other supreme shanti mantras: Saha Navavatu (the teacher-student peace), Purnamadah Purnamidam (the fullness mantra), Asato Ma Sadgamaya (lead me from untruth to truth), Sarve Bhavantu Sukhinah (universal happiness), Sarvesham Svastir-bhavatu (welfare for all), and Bhadram Karnebhih Shrinuyama (the auspicious-hearing mantra). (Source: Yajurveda 36.17; Upanishadic shanti mantra corpus.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the meaning of Om Dyauh Shanti — the opening universal peace mantra?
seoFaqs.answer: The opening of the Shanti Path is the supreme universal-peace mantra of the Yajurveda (36.17): Om Dyauh Shantir-antarikshag Shantih, prithivi shantir-apah shantir-oshadhayah shantih; vanaspatayah Shantir-vishve devah Shantir-Brahma Shantih, sarvag Shantih Shantir-eva Shantih sa ma Shantir-edhi — 'Om! May there be peace in the heavens (Dyauh — the celestial sphere), peace in the atmosphere (antariksha), peace on earth (prithivi), peace in the waters (apah), peace in the medicinal herbs (oshadhayah), peace in the plants and trees (vanaspatayah), peace in the Vishve-devas (all the gods), peace in Brahman, peace in everything (sarvam), peace itself be peace, and may that peace come to me.' This mantra invokes peace in nine domains progressively expanding outward and then inward — finally settling in Brahman and in oneself. It is the supreme Vedic ecological prayer. (Source: Yajurveda 36.17.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the meaning of Saha Navavatu — the teacher-student peace mantra?
seoFaqs.answer: The Saha Navavatu mantra is the shanti mantra of the Krishna Yajurveda Taittiriya tradition, recited at the beginning and end of every Upanishadic study session: Om Saha navavatu, saha nau bhunaktu, saha viryam karavavahai; tejasvi navadhitam-astu, ma vidvishavahai; Om Shantih Shantih Shantih — 'Om! May He (the Lord) protect us both (teacher and student) together; may He nourish us both together; may we both work together (in pursuit of knowledge) with great vigour; may our study be brilliant and effective (tejasvi); may we never quarrel (or hate) each other (ma vidvishavahai). Om Peace, Peace, Peace.' This is the foundational mantra of the Hindu guru-shishya parampara — recited together by guru and disciple before opening any Vedanta text and before closing any study session. It establishes the spirit of cooperative learning, mutual respect and freedom from intellectual quarrelling. (Source: Krishna Yajurveda Taittiriya Upanishad shanti mantra.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the meaning of Purnamadah Purnamidam — the fullness mantra?
seoFaqs.answer: The Purnamadah Purnamidam mantra is one of the most profound philosophical statements in the entire Veda — the shanti mantra of the Brihadaranyaka Upanishad and the Isha Upanishad: Om Purnam-adah Purnam-idam, Purnat Purnam-udachyate; Purnasya Purnam-adaya, Purnam-eva-vashishyate — 'That (Brahman, the unmanifest) is Full (Purna). This (creation, the manifest) is Full. From the Full, the Full has come forth. Even after taking the Full from the Full, the Full alone remains.' This is the supreme statement of the infinite fullness of Brahman — that the Absolute does not diminish even when the entire creation issues forth from it, because both cause and effect are equally infinite. The mantra teaches that Brahman is purna (complete, infinite, full) and that the manifest universe is also purna (because it is non-different from Brahman). It is one of the supreme Vedic mantras on infinity. (Source: Isha Upanishad shanti mantra; Brihadaranyaka Upanishad 5.1.1.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the meaning of Asato Ma Sadgamaya — the famous Brihadaranyaka prayer?
seoFaqs.answer: The Asato Ma Sadgamaya mantra is one of the most loved Sanskrit prayers worldwide, drawn from the Brihadaranyaka Upanishad (1.3.28): Om Asato ma sadgamaya, Tamaso ma jyotir-gamaya, Mrityor-ma amritam gamaya, Om Shantih Shantih Shantih — 'Lead me from the unreal to the Real (asato sat), lead me from darkness to Light (tamas to jyoti), lead me from death to Immortality (mrityu to amrita); Om Peace Peace Peace.' This three-fold prayer encompasses the entire Vedantic journey: from epistemological illusion (asat) to truth (sat), from spiritual ignorance (tamas) to wisdom (jyoti), and from the bondage of mortality (mrityu) to the freedom of immortality (amrita). It is one of the most quoted Hindu mantras in the world, recited at every interfaith gathering, at the United Nations, in classrooms and ashrams worldwide. (Source: Brihadaranyaka Upanishad 1.3.28.)

seoFaqs.question: When should the Shanti Path be recited and what are its benefits?
seoFaqs.answer: The Shanti Path is recited at the beginning and end of every traditional Hindu spiritual activity — puja, havan, kirtan, Vedanta lecture, satsang, daily sandhya, marriage, mundane samskara, namakaran, upanayana, grih-pravesha, and at the close of every Upanishadic study session. Tradition assigns the following blessings: (1) establishment of universal peace and harmony in the environment (heavens, atmosphere, earth, waters, plants, devas), (2) removal of the three types of suffering — adhyatmika (within oneself, body and mind), adhibhautika (from other beings) and adhidaivika (from the elements and devas), (3) protection of the worshipper through the surrounding peace-field, (4) the merit of universal goodwill (Sarve Bhavantu Sukhinah), (5) cooperative learning between teacher and student (Saha Navavatu), (6) Vedantic insight into the fullness of Brahman (Purnamadah Purnamidam), (7) progress from ignorance to wisdom (Asato Ma Sadgamaya), and (8) auspicious hearing, seeing and use of the body for divine purposes (Bhadram Karnebhih Shrinuyama). (Source: Vedic shanti mantra tradition; classical puja paddhati.)
